turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: I have the opinion of the Court this morning in Case 23-939, Trump versus United States. A federal grand jury indicted former President Donald Trump for conduct that occurred during the final days of his presidency following the November 2020 election. The indictment alleges that after he lost that election Trump conspired to overturn its results. According to the indictment, that conspiracy involved trying to convince state officials that election fraud required them to change the results in their states, attempting to get the Justice Department to join that effort, organizing alternative slates of electors to cast votes in Trump’s favor, trying to get the vice president to derail the certification proceeding, and encouraging a crowd to gather on January 6 on the mall. Members of that crowd eventually stormed the Capitol and halted the certification proceeding. Trump moved to dismiss the indictment. He contended that all of the alleged conduct properly characterized qualified as official conduct of the president, and he argued that the president is absolutely immune from prosecution for such conduct. The lower courts rejected that immunity claim. We granted certiorari to consider whether and to what extent a former president enjoys presidential immunity from criminal prosecution for his official actions. This is the first time that courts have been called upon to answer that question because no president in our nation’s history has ever been prosecuted before. To determine whether a president has any protection from criminal prosecution for his official conduct, we therefore have to look broadly at the role of the president and the nature of his power under our system of government. Article II of the Constitution vests the executive power in a president of the United States. The president is the only official who alone composes an entire branch of government. He has duties of unrivaled gravity and breadth. To name just a few, he must command the nation’s armed forces, faithfully execute the laws passed by Congress, and appoint federal judges, including the justices of this Court. He also has vast foreign relations responsibilities, appointing ambassadors, meeting foreign leaders, overseeing international diplomacy, and he is generally responsible for the many departments and agencies within the Executive Branch. Some of the president’s powers are granted exclusively to him by the Constitution. Congress and the courts have no authority with respect to those powers. So when the president exercises them, his decision on the matter is conclusive and preclusive. For example, the Constitution vests the power to grant reprieves and pardons only in the president. Congress cannot impair or control his pardon power and courts cannot review his decision to pardon someone. It follows then that if congressional acts cannot apply to the president’s pardons and if courts cannot review them, then the president must be immune from prosecution for his pardoning decisions. But of course not all the actions that the president takes are within his exclusive sphere of constitutional authority. More often the president acts in areas where he and Congress share concurrent authority. In one of our prior decision, Nixon against Fitzgerald, we decided that when the president takes official actions including in those areas, he is immune from civil damages liability. We recognize that the president was required to make the most sensitive and far-reaching decisions entrusted to any official under our constitutional system. There accordingly exist the greatest public interest in providing him with a maximum ability to deal fearlessly and impartially with the duties of his office. We wrote that if the president were making decisions under constant threat that he may be sued for those decisions in the future, his decision making could be distorted by the concern that he may be held personally liable for his official decisions after he leaves office. So we reasoned in Fitzgerald that the public interest in ensuring that the president’s decision making is not distorted in this way requires recognizing presidential immunity from damages liability. Fitzgerald was about civil damages liability and this is a criminal case. But we have recognized certain protections for the president even in criminal cases where a prosecutor has sought evidence from the president. When President Nixon was issued a subpoena for instance, we held that although he did not have absolute immunity from responding to the subpoena, a presumptive privilege protected the president’s communications. That privilege we reasoned is necessary to enable the effective discharge of the president’s duties. After all the president must be able to communicate freely with his advisers who assist him in the performance of those duties. Prosecuting the president for his official conduct poses a far greater threat of intrusion on the Executive Branch than merely seeking evidence in his possession. That threat is akin to what led us to recognize presidential immunity from damages in Fitzgerald. If the president must make official decisions under constant threat to trial, judgment and imprisonment may befall him upon his exit from office, there is great risk that his decision making will be distorted while he is in office. A president inclined to take one course of action based on the public interest may instead opt for another, apprehensive that if not he will be prosecuted by his successor in the future. The framers did not envision that kind of burden on the president’s official decision making. They instead envisioned an executive who would act vigorously and decisively to steadily administer the laws, protect the community against foreign attacks, and secure liberty. At the same time, there is a compelling public interest in fair and effective law enforcement. The president who is charged with enforcing federal criminal laws is not above those laws himself. Taking into account these competing considerations, we conclude that the separation of powers requires that the president be at least presumptively immune from criminal prosecutions for actions within the outer perimeter of his official responsibility. If presumptive protection for the president is necessary to enable the effective discharge of his powers when a prosecutor merely seeks evidence in the president’s possession, it is certainly necessary when the prosecutor seeks to charge, try and imprison the president himself. The president must therefore be immune from prosecution for an official act unless the government can show that applying a criminal prohibition to that act would pose no dangers of intrusion on the authority and functions of the Executive Branch. On the other hand, no immunity applies to a president’s unofficial conduct. The president is not immune because he is the president. Rather immunity exists for the president’s official conduct to ensure that he is able to undertake his constitutionally designated functions fearlessly and fairly. So deciding whether a particular prosecution of the former president can proceed first requires determining whether he is being prosecuted for official or unofficial conduct. But drawing that distinction can be difficult given the breadth of the president’s responsibilities under the Constitution and the laws of the United States. A number of critical features of the case as it comes to us are highly pertinent in moving forward. First, neither the District Court, nor the Court of Appeals analyzed the conduct alleged in the indictment to determine which aspects included official or unofficial conduct, how to separate the two if they are merged, or how to apply in appropriate standard of immunity. Second, how to distinguish between official and unofficial conduct and the application of the chosen approach was not briefed before us. Third, this matter has been handled expeditiously at the government’s urging. Fourth, as noted, this is the first time since the Constitution was adopted that legal and factual issues concerning a criminal prosecution of a president have arisen. Fifth, there is accordingly no precedent directly on point and little that is pertinent. Sixth, the issues are plainly quite difficult, particularly given the nature and breadth of the conduct alleged. And seventh, the issues are momentous, especially since their resolution involves not just questions of immunity, but of the separation of powers generally. We often explain that we are a Court of review, not first view, and stress the critical importance of guidance from litigants and the rulings of the Courts below. That properly limits the issues we can resolve at this point. The Court’s opinion today offers initial guidance on how to differentiate between a president’s official and unofficial actions and how to categorize some of the conduct alleged in the indictment. The indictment’s allegations cover a broad range of conduct in great detail. The alleged conspiracy involved Trump’s interactions with the acting attorney general and the Justice Department, the vice president, state legislators and election officials, his campaign staff and other private parties and the general public. Given the nature of the president’s official relationship to the offices held by some of those individuals, some of the indictment’s allegations can be readily categorized as involving official conduct. When the president discusses the vice president’s constitutional and statutory responsibilities with him, for instance, he engages in official conduct for which he is at least presumptively immune from prosecution. We do not go on at this point to decide whether that presumption is overcome on the facts before us. Many of the indictment’s allegations are difficult to categorize without guidance from the parties and the courts below. We therefore remand the case to the lower courts to address and determine those and other pertinent issues in the first instance. Dissenting opinions have also been filed in this case. They predict that the Court’s decision will lead to dire consequences. But in our view those predictions are wholly disproportionate to what the Court actually does today, recognize that the separation of powers requires some presidential immunity from criminal prosecution for the president’s official conduct and provide guidance on how that immunity applies to some of the alleged conduct in this case. Before laying out those basic principles, the Court sends the matter back to the lower courts for careful consideration and application of those principles to the indictment in this case. The dissenting opinions take the position that even though the president composes an entire branch of government and must make numerous sensitive and far-reaching decisions every day during his term in office, he enjoys no protection whatever from prosecution for those myriad decisions. Recognizing immunity they predict will let the president freely commit crimes. What the dissents ignore the possibility that without immunity, every time a new president who disagrees with the decisions of his predecessor and his office, he will have free reign to scrutinize every moment of the prior four or eight years and prosecute his outgoing predecessor, and his successor might do the same to him in turn. The end result would be an Executive Branch that cannibalizes itself, imposing hesitancy and weakness on a president, wholly contrary to the framers’ visions of the demands of the office. In his famous farewell address our first president cautioned the nation that just such "alternate domination of one faction over another, sharpened by the spirit of revenge would lead to a frightful despotism." This Court’s decision therefore focuses on the enduring consequences of this case on the structure of our republic, not on any present dispute. The immunity we recognize today does not just protect any particular president; it protects the institution of the presidency. Work remains to be done in this case and the courts below, but this much we can say, the president enjoys no immunity for his unofficial acts and not everything the president does is official. The president is not above the law. But Congress may not criminalize the president’s conduct in carrying out the responsibilities of the Executive Branch under the Constitution, and the system of separated powers designed by the framers has always demanded an energetic independent executive. The president therefore may not be prosecuted for exercising his core constitutional powers and he is entitled at a minimum to a presumptive immunity from prosecution for his official acts. That immunity applies equally to all occupants of the Oval Office regardless of politics, policy or party. The judgment of the Court of Appeals is vacated and the case is remanded for further proceedings. Justice Barrett joined the opinion of the Court except as to Part 3C. Justices Thomas and Barrett each filed a concurring opinion. Justice Sotomayor has filed a dissenting opinion, in which Justices Kagan and Jackson joined. Justice Jackson filed a dissenting opinion.
